skill.name: building-commands
skill.description: Create, update, validate, and harden Claude Code slash commands with correct YAML frontmatter, argument documentation, tool permissions, and model field guidance.
skill.summary: Build and maintain Claude Code slash commands with validation and safety guidance.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, model-name, and documentation patterns, but most are markdown examples, checklists, or anti-patterns rather than hidden execution paths. The skill still has medium risk because it is community-authored, allows Bash/Write/Edit, and helps generate Bash-capable command files that can affect the filesystem if users approve unsafe workflows. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden network calls, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Bash-capable command generation requires user review
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches commands to use Bash and includes workflow examples such as git add, commit, and push. This is legitimate for command automation, but generated commands can execute external programs and should be reviewed before use. Static external-command findings are mostly true as capability indicators, not evidence of malicious execution. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: Multiple files intentionally document Bash-enabled command patterns, and the skill itself declares Bash in allowed-tools. The context is educational and workflow-oriented, so the risk is moderate rather than high.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly supports Bash-capable slash command creation. The reviewed content frames this as legitimate automation with safety guidance, not malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Generated commands may write to project or user command directories
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs creation of project-level, user-level, and plugin-level command files. This filesystem access is expected for a command-building skill, but user-level command paths can persist workflows beyond the current project. Confidence: 0.72. Confidence reasoning: File locations and write targets are documented directly, while no hidden traversal or unauthorized file access was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is part of the stated purpose. It needs caution because user-level command files can persist, but the evidence does not show malicious writes.

low_findings.title: Static weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts are mostly documentation false positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged model aliases, markdown extensions, validation wording, and command examples as weak cryptography or reconnaissance. Reviewed context shows these are documentation terms and command-quality checks, not cryptographic implementations or host enumeration logic. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The cited locations discuss Claude model names, markdown files, YAML validation, and command checklists; no crypto API or reconnaissance payload was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched strings are documentation and validation terms. There is no evidence of cryptographic code or system reconnaissance intent.

low_findings.title: Single external documentation URL
low_findings.description: The skill links to Anthropic model documentation so users can check current model names. No code fetches this URL automatically, and no data is sent to it by the skill. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The only reviewed network evidence is a static markdown link, not executable network code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a documentation link only. No network library, request code, or exfiltration path was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented unsafe shell anti-patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference guide includes examples of eval, rm -rf with variables, and unquoted shell input as patterns to avoid. These examples are intentionally labeled dangerous and followed by safer guidance, but they should not be copied into generated commands. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The dangerous strings are present and semantically risky if reused, yet the surrounding text clearly presents them as anti-patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous commands are real shell anti-patterns. Their documentation context reduces severity because the skill warns against them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path traversal discussed as validation requirement
dangerous_patterns.description: The checklist and skill mention path traversal prevention when commands accept file paths. This is a security control recommendation, not evidence that the skill performs traversal. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The cited text explicitly tells command authors to sanitize paths and prevent traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static path traversal findings map to defensive guidance. I found no code path that constructs traversal paths for access.

content.value_statement: Creating slash commands is error-prone when YAML fields, tool permissions, and arguments are inconsistent. This skill guides command design, validation, migration, and safety review for Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Designs Claude Code slash command structure with YAML frontmatter and Markdown bodies.
Explains required and optional fields such as description, allowed-tools, argument-hint, and model.
Provides templates for arguments, workflows, examples, constraints, and security notes.
Validates command files with a Python helper for naming, YAML syntax, model fields, and documentation.
Guides migration from short model aliases to version aliases or full model IDs.
Recommends safer tool permissions and Bash input validation patterns.
limitations: It targets Claude Code slash commands, not general CLI frameworks.
It cannot guarantee that generated Bash workflows are safe without human review.
Model names in examples may become outdated and should be checked against current documentation.
The validator focuses on structure and common risks, not full semantic security analysis.

use_cases.title: Create a Team Command
use_cases.description: Define a reusable slash command for reviews, test runs, deployments, or documentation tasks.

use_cases.title: Repair Broken Commands
use_cases.description: Update invalid model fields, missing descriptions, argument hints, and outdated command formats.

use_cases.title: Audit Command Permissions
use_cases.description: Review command files for excessive tools, unsafe Bash patterns, weak argument handling, and missing safeguards.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code slash command named run-tests. It should run the project test suite and report failures clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Add Arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a slash command that accepts a file path and an optional mode. Include argument-hint, argument documentation, and examples.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Existing Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this command file for frontmatter, model field, allowed-tools, argument handling, and workflow clarity. Suggest exact edits.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a Bash Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Bash-enabled slash command. Minimize allowed tools, validate file paths, quote shell input, and document destructive safeguards.

output_examples.input: Create a slash command for reviewing pull requests.
output_examples.output: A command design with a clear description, PR number argument, minimal required tools, workflow steps, and example usage.

output_examples.input: Fix a command that uses model: haiku.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to remove the model field or replace it with a valid Claude version alias, plus validation steps.

output_examples.input: Review a command that uses Bash with file arguments.
output_examples.output: A safety checklist covering path validation, shell quoting, tool minimization, and confirmation before destructive actions.

best_practices: Start with the smallest allowed-tools set and add Bash only when the workflow truly needs it.
Document every argument with expected format, required status, defaults, and example invocations.
Validate generated commands before use, especially commands that write files or run shell commands.
anti_patterns: Do not use short model aliases such as haiku, sonnet, or opus in command frontmatter.
Do not pass user arguments into Bash without validation, quoting, and clear constraints.
Do not create broad automation commands that can delete, overwrite, deploy, or push without safeguards.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps create and maintain Claude Code slash commands with frontmatter, arguments, workflows, examples, and validation guidance.

faq.question: Does it execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and a validator script. Generated slash commands may execute tools later if users approve them.

faq.question: Why does it mention Bash?
faq.answer: Many slash commands automate tests, git operations, or build tasks. The skill also explains when Bash needs extra review.

faq.question: Can it fix model field errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains that commands should use Claude version aliases or full IDs, not short aliases.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security-sensitive commands?
faq.answer: It can guide hardening, but destructive or Bash-heavy commands still require human review before use.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported marketplace tools.
